name: Deployment Frequency (DF)
shortDescription: How often code is successfully released to production.
measurement: Count of successful production deployment events per time window from CI/CD logs (GitHub Actions, GitLab, Jenkins, Spinnaker, Argo). DORA "elite" is approximately on-demand or multiple times per day.
difficultyJustification: Pure event count; main challenge is consistent definition of "production" and "deployment."

name: Lead Time for Changes (LTC)
shortDescription: Time from code commit (or PR open) to that change running in production.
measurement: timestamp(deploy) - timestamp(commit) aggregated as median or percentile. Computed by GitLab Value Stream Analytics, LinearB, DX, and Jellyfish.
difficultyJustification: Requires joining VCS + CI + deploy data; commit-time vs. PR-open-time choice changes the number materially.

name: Change Failure Rate (CFR)
shortDescription: % of deployments that cause a production incident, rollback, or hotfix.
measurement: failed_deployments / total_deployments. Requires linking incidents/rollbacks back to specific deployments.
difficultyJustification: "Failure" definition varies (incident? rollback? hotfix within X hours?), and incident-to-deploy linkage is often manual.

name: Mean Time to Restore/Recover (MTTR - DORA)
shortDescription: Average time from production incident start to service restored.
measurement: sum(downtime) / number_of_incidents. Sourced from incident-management tools such as PagerDuty, Opsgenie, and ServiceNow. DORA elite is under 1 hour.
difficultyJustification: Skewed by outliers; depends on incident-start definition (alert fire vs. customer impact start). Median/p90 is often more honest than mean.

name: Reliability (5th DORA metric)
shortDescription: Composite signal of whether services meet their reliability targets (SLOs) over time.
measurement: Usually tracked as the share of service-level objectives met across services in a reporting window; some teams instead summarize weighted error-budget consumption.
difficultyJustification: Requires a mature SLO program; aggregation across services is subjective.

name: Cyclomatic Complexity (McCabe)
shortDescription: Number of linearly independent paths through a function: 1 + number_of_conditional_branches.
measurement: Static analysis: SonarQube, ESLint complexity rules, radon (Python), lizard, PMD. Computed per function and summed.
difficultyJustification: Deterministic and tool-supported; interpretation thresholds (>10 risky, >20 high-risk) vary by language.

name: Cognitive Complexity
shortDescription: How hard code is for a human to understand (penalizes nesting and breaks in linear flow).
measurement: SonarSource's algorithm in SonarQube; differs from McCabe by weighting nested structures.
difficultyJustification: Tool support makes calculation straightforward; the metric itself is opinionated.

name: Code Coverage (line / statement / branch / condition)
shortDescription: % of code (lines, branches, or conditions) executed by tests.
measurement: Coverage instrumentation: JaCoCo, Coverage.py, Istanbul/nyc, gcov, Codecov, Coveralls. Branch coverage stricter than line.
difficultyJustification: Calculation is straightforward, but interpreting the result correctly is difficult. 100% coverage does not imply correctness; it is necessary-but-not-sufficient.

name: Technical Debt Ratio (SQALE)
shortDescription: Ratio of estimated remediation cost to total development cost. SonarQube: remediation_cost / development_cost.
measurement: SonarQube SQALE model assigns minutes per issue; development cost is approximated as LOC x cost_per_line.
difficultyJustification: Depends on rule set and remediation-effort estimates baked into the tool; cross-org comparisons are weak.

name: Code Duplication
shortDescription: % of code that is duplicated (clone detection).
measurement: SonarQube duplication detector, PMD CPD, jscpd. Reported as duplicated_lines / total_lines.
difficultyJustification: Tool-driven; threshold for "duplicate" (token count) is configurable.

name: Code Churn
shortDescription: Lines added/changed/deleted over a window, often within N days of being written.
measurement: Git log analysis; tools like CodeScene, GitPrime/Pluralsight Flow. High churn correlates with defect rate (Nagappan & Ball, MSR).
difficultyJustification: Trivial to compute; interpreting "good vs. bad" churn requires baseline.

name: Build Success Rate
shortDescription: % of CI builds passing on first attempt.
measurement: successful_builds / total_builds from CI APIs such as Jenkins, GitHub Actions, Buildkite, and CircleCI. Elite teams often target >= 90% on the default branch.
difficultyJustification: Direct from CI logs; partition by branch (feature vs. main) for fair signal.

name: Pipeline Duration
shortDescription: Wall-clock time from pipeline trigger to completion.
measurement: CI platform analytics measuring wall-clock time from pipeline trigger to final completion, typically tracked as median and p95 with queue time broken out separately.
difficultyJustification: Build/queue split aids diagnosis.

name: Queue Time
shortDescription: Time builds wait before an executor picks them up.
measurement: CI scheduler timestamps, usually captured directly from Jenkins, GitHub Actions, Buildkite, or similar CI platforms.
difficultyJustification: Some CI tools expose it natively; others require log scraping.

name: Flaky Test Rate
shortDescription: % of tests that have failed and then passed without code change in a window.
measurement: Cross-reference test-run history with git diffs; tools: Buildkite Test Analytics, CircleCI Test Insights, Atlassian Flakinator, Datadog CI Visibility. Empirically, Google reports ~16% of test failures are flaky; Microsoft ~13%.
difficultyJustification: Requires correlating runs to code state; many CIs do not natively distinguish flake from real failure.

name: Mean Time to Green (MTTG)
shortDescription: Time from PR open until CI is green.
measurement: Join pull request timestamps with CI run results to measure time from PR open until the default required checks are green.
difficultyJustification: Depends on flake handling and rerun policies.

name: Vulnerability Density
shortDescription: Open vulnerabilities per asset (or per KLOC).
measurement: Counts from SCA/SAST/DAST scanners (Snyk, Dependabot, Tenable, Qualys, Wiz) divided by inventory.
difficultyJustification: Sensitive to scanner coverage and CVE deduplication; not comparable across tools.

name: MTTR for CVEs / Mean Time to Remediate
shortDescription: Average time from vulnerability detection to verified fix.
measurement: sum(close_time - detect_time) / count, sliced by severity. Edgescan's 2023 report puts industry-wide critical MTTR at approximately 65 days.
difficultyJustification: Detection timestamp varies by source (scan run vs. CVE disclosure).

name: Mean Time to Patch (MTTP) / Patch Lag
shortDescription: Gap between vendor patch release and deployment in the environment.
measurement: Patch-deploy timestamp minus vendor-release timestamp; tracked in patch-management tools (WSUS, Tanium, JAMF).
difficultyJustification: Requires authoritative inventory and vendor patch metadata.

name: Patch Compliance Rate
shortDescription: % of in-scope assets within patch-SLA.
measurement: Asset-level patch compliance states aggregated by severity tier, business unit, or environment against a defined remediation SLA.
difficultyJustification: Asset inventory completeness is usually the bottleneck.

name: Scanner / Inventory Coverage
shortDescription: % of assets actually scanned within the last cycle.
measurement: (asset inventory intersection scanned assets) / asset inventory, usually measured per scan cycle to show how much of the known environment is actually being assessed.
difficultyJustification: Shadow IT and ephemeral cloud assets degrade accuracy.

name: MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)
shortDescription: Average operational time before a non-repairable component fails.
measurement: total_operational_time / number_of_failures. Used for hardware-style or replaceable components.
difficultyJustification: Hard for software (often poorly defined; software is typically repairable, so MTBF is more appropriate).

name: MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)
shortDescription: Average uptime between failures for repairable systems.
measurement: MTTF + MTTR or total_uptime / number_of_failures. Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
difficultyJustification: Easily gamed by raising the threshold of what counts as an incident.

name: MTTD (Mean Time to Detect)
shortDescription: Time from failure onset until monitoring/team becomes aware.
measurement: sum(alert_time - failure_start_time) / count. Needs ground truth on when failure actually began, often from forensic log review rather than alerts.
difficultyJustification: True failure-start is rarely known precisely; many teams understate MTTD by using alert-fire as start.

name: MTTA (Mean Time to Acknowledge)
shortDescription: Time from alert page to human acknowledgement.
measurement: PagerDuty, Opsgenie, or similar incident-management analytics comparing page time with acknowledgement time.
difficultyJustification: Direct from incident tool.

name: SLI (Service Level Indicator)
shortDescription: Quantitative measure of service behavior, e.g., good_requests / total_requests, p99 latency.
measurement: Computed from logs, metrics (Prometheus, Datadog, New Relic), or synthetic checks.
difficultyJustification: Choosing "good event" predicates and request scopes is the hard part.

name: SLO Compliance
shortDescription: % of measurement window in which SLI met its objective.
measurement: Burn-rate calculation against an SLO target (e.g., 99.9%). Tools: Sloth, Nobl9, Datadog SLO, Grafana SLO.
difficultyJustification: Definition of valid traffic and exclusions (planned maintenance) is contentious.

name: Error Budget
shortDescription: Allowed unreliability per window = 1 - SLO. For 99.9% over 30 days, that is approximately 43 minutes.
measurement: Derived from the SLO as 1 - target reliability, then tracked through burn-rate alerts and cumulative consumption over the reporting window.
difficultyJustification: Easy formula, but operational use requires organizational buy-in.

name: Toil Fraction
shortDescription: % of engineer time spent on manual, repetitive ops work.
measurement: Self-reported time tracking, ticket tagging, or calendar analysis estimating the share of engineer time spent on repetitive operational work. The Google SRE book suggests keeping this below 50%.
difficultyJustification: Almost entirely self-reported; subject to recall and definition bias.

name: PR Cycle Time
shortDescription: End-to-end time from PR opened to merged (sometimes to deploy).
measurement: Git host APIs or engineering analytics tools measuring elapsed time from PR creation to merge, and sometimes onward to deployment.
difficultyJustification: Sensitive to draft-PR practices and squash-merge conventions.

name: PR Review Turnaround / Pickup Time
shortDescription: Hours from PR opened to first substantive review.
measurement: Git host webhook or API timestamps comparing PR creation time with the first substantive review event.
difficultyJustification: Often the largest hidden contributor to cycle time.

name: PR Size
shortDescription: Lines or files changed per PR.
measurement: Git diff statistics such as lines changed or files touched per PR, often analyzed against review speed, merge time, or downstream defect rates.
difficultyJustification: LOC is noisy across languages.

name: Throughput
shortDescription: Issues, stories, or PRs completed per unit time.
measurement: Counts of issues, stories, tickets, or PRs completed per time window using sources such as Jira, Linear, or GitHub Projects.
difficultyJustification: Easy to count, Hard to interpret (story points are subjective).

name: Work in Progress (WIP)
shortDescription: Items concurrently in-flight per team or person.
measurement: Pulled from the issue tracker. Little's Law: WIP = throughput x cycle_time.
difficultyJustification: Definition of "in progress" status must be agreed.

name: Rework Rate
shortDescription: % of code changed within N days of being merged.
measurement: Git churn analysis identifying the percentage of code that is modified again within a chosen window after merge, often using tools such as CodeScene or GitPrime.
difficultyJustification: Window choice matters; legitimate iteration looks like rework.

name: Sprint Goal Completion Rate
shortDescription: % of committed sprint scope delivered.
measurement: Jira, Linear, or similar sprint reports comparing committed scope with completed scope at sprint end.
difficultyJustification: Straightforward to compute, but teams can game it by under-committing or redefining scope mid-sprint.

name: Latency (request)
shortDescription: Time from request to response, usually as p50/p95/p99 distribution.
measurement: Latency distributions from APM or observability systems such as Prometheus histograms, Datadog, or OpenTelemetry, usually reported as p50, p95, and p99.
difficultyJustification: The long tail (p99) is the meaningful number.

name: Traffic / Request Rate (R in RED, Traffic Golden Signal)
shortDescription: Requests per second served.
measurement: APM or observability counter metrics, usually expressed as requests per second over time.
difficultyJustification: Simple counter-based metric, though teams still need to define which requests count toward the total.

name: Error Rate (E in RED, Errors Golden Signal)
shortDescription: Failed requests / total requests.
measurement: Error counters from logs or metrics, usually calculated as failed requests divided by total requests, with 5xx responses, timeouts, and uncaught exceptions included.
difficultyJustification: Definition of "error" needs care (4xx often excluded).

name: Saturation (Golden Signals, S in USE)
shortDescription: How "full" a resource is; backlog/queue depth.
measurement: Resource-specific pressure signals such as CPU run queue, connection-pool exhaustion, GC pressure, queue depth, or Kafka consumer lag.
difficultyJustification: Different resources have different saturation proxies.

name: Utilization (U in USE)
shortDescription: Fraction of time a resource is busy.
measurement: Host, container, or service-level metrics from tools such as top, vmstat, Prometheus node_exporter, or cloud monitoring systems.
difficultyJustification: Usually easy to collect, but interpretation depends on the resource type and the difference between busy time and saturation.

name: CPU / Memory Utilization
shortDescription: % of compute/memory consumed.
measurement: Host, VM, or container metrics from tools such as node_exporter, cAdvisor, CloudWatch, or eBPF-based monitors, usually broken down by CPU percentage and memory working set or RSS.
difficultyJustification: but containerized memory (cache vs. RSS vs. working set) is nuanced.

name: Availability / Uptime
shortDescription: % of time service is up over a window.
measurement: Synthetic monitoring, uptime checks, or SLI-based calculations measuring the percentage of time a service is available over a reporting window.
difficultyJustification: "Up" definition (does the synthetic probe match user experience?) is contestable.

name: Defect Density
shortDescription: Defects per KLOC or per module.
measurement: Bug tracker counts divided by KLOC, module size, or another chosen software-size denominator.
difficultyJustification: Bug-reporting practices vary; LOC denominator is noisy.

name: Defect Escape Rate
shortDescription: % of defects that reach production vs. caught pre-prod.
measurement: defects_found_in_prod / (defects_found_in_prod + defects_found_pre_prod) over a release.
difficultyJustification: Requires consistent bug triage and time-window normalization.

name: Test Pass Rate
shortDescription: % of executed tests that pass.
measurement: CI test reports comparing passed tests with the total executed tests in a run or time window.
difficultyJustification: Can hide quality issues if flakes or skips are high.

name: Mutation Score
shortDescription: % of injected code mutations that tests catch. Stronger signal than line coverage.
measurement: Mutation-testing tools such as Stryker, PIT, or Mutmut inject code changes and report the percentage of mutants that the test suite detects.
difficultyJustification: Computationally expensive; integrating into CI is non-trivial.

name: Construct Validity
shortDescription: Whether the metric actually measures the conceptual attribute it claims to (e.g., does "PR count" measure "productivity"?).
measurement: Theoretical mapping of the construct to indicators, then convergent/discriminant validity tests against alternative measures. Triangulation; expert review (Ralph et al., 2022; Kaner & Bond, 2004).
difficultyJustification: Requires defining the construct precisely and finding independent measures; rarely done in industry dashboards.

name: Content Validity
shortDescription: Whether the metric covers the full breadth of the construct (e.g., does a code-quality score include security, maintainability, and performance?).
measurement: Domain-expert panel review; mapping metric components against a documented taxonomy such as ISO/IEC 25010 sub-characteristics.
difficultyJustification: Subjective but tractable through structured review.

name: Criterion / Predictive Validity
shortDescription: Whether the metric predicts an external outcome of interest (e.g., does cyclomatic complexity predict defect rate?).
measurement: Correlation, regression, or causal-inference tests against the criterion variable on historical data.
difficultyJustification: Needs historical labeled data and care to avoid leakage.

name: Reliability (Repeatability)
shortDescription: Whether re-measuring the same artifact under the same conditions yields the same value.
measurement: Test-retest, inter-rater agreement (for example Cohen's kappa or ICC) for human-judged metrics; deterministic re-runs for tool-based metrics.
difficultyJustification: Easy for deterministic tools, Hard for human-judgment metrics like "code-review quality."

name: Stability / Variance over Time
shortDescription: Whether the metric drifts when the underlying system has not changed.
measurement: Statistical process control: rolling mean and standard deviation; flag if coefficient of variation > threshold. Control charts (Shewhart, EWMA).
difficultyJustification: Requires a stable baseline; seasonality complicates.

name: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)
shortDescription: Whether real change is larger than random fluctuation.
measurement: SNR = (mean change attributable to intervention) / (standard deviation of background noise). Power analysis to determine minimum detectable effect.
difficultyJustification: Requires controlled experimentation or interrupted-time-series; rare in operational metrics.

name: Sensitivity
shortDescription: How responsive the metric is to real changes in the underlying construct.
measurement: Apply a known stimulus (e.g., introduce a deliberate defect, slow a service) and observe metric delta; compute elasticity.
difficultyJustification: Requires fault injection or chaos engineering.

name: Discriminative Power
shortDescription: Whether the metric can distinguish high-performing from low-performing units.
measurement: Compare metric distributions across known-different groups (e.g., teams classified independently as elite vs. baseline) using effect-size statistics (Cohen's d, AUC). Listed in IEEE Std 1061 / Schneidewind 1991.
difficultyJustification: Requires ground-truth labels.

name: Goodhart Susceptibility ("Gameability")
shortDescription: How easily the metric can be manipulated without delivering the underlying value.
measurement: Adversarial review: list the cheapest actions that would move the metric, and rate whether they correlate with the construct. Empirically: watch for sudden distributional shifts after the metric becomes a target. Goodhart-style typology (Manheim & Garrabrant): regressive, extremal, causal, adversarial.
difficultyJustification: No standard quantitative method; depends on expert red-teaming.

name: Observer / Hawthorne Effect Magnitude
shortDescription: How much the act of measuring changes the behavior being measured.
measurement: Compare observed-versus-unobserved baselines; longitudinal decay analysis where novelty typically wears off in 1-4 weeks; subtle rollout designs such as concealed feature flags via Statsig.
difficultyJustification: Ethically and practically tricky; usually requires some period of concealed or minimally obtrusive measurement.

name: Bias in Collection
shortDescription: Systematic skew due to who or what is observed (e.g., LOC penalizes terse languages; coverage tools miss generated code; survey responders are not representative).
measurement: Tool-comparison studies; demographic / language / repo-segmented breakdowns to detect disparate measurement.
difficultyJustification: Requires multiple independent measurement instruments.

name: Correlation / Collinearity with Other Metrics
shortDescription: Whether the metric is redundant with existing ones.
measurement: Pearson/Spearman correlation matrix; Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) > 5 indicates collinearity; Principal Component Analysis to find latent dimensions.
difficultyJustification: Standard statistical procedure once a metric inventory exists.

name: Coverage / Blind-Spot Score
shortDescription: Whether important parts of the construct are unmeasured (the "dark matter" of metrics).
measurement: Map metrics onto a reference framework (SPACE dimensions, ISO/IEC 25010 categories, DORA velocity + stability, Four Golden Signals) and count gaps.
difficultyJustification: Requires an agreed framework.

name: Actionability
shortDescription: Whether a change in the metric points to a clear, owned action.
measurement: For each metric ask: (a) is there a defined owner? (b) is there a documented intervention if it moves? (c) has the intervention been validated to move the metric? Score 0-3.
difficultyJustification: Subjective but easy to operationalize as a governance checklist.

name: Leading vs. Lagging Classification
shortDescription: Whether the metric reveals future outcomes or only confirms past ones; balanced programs need both.
measurement: Lag analysis: cross-correlation between the candidate metric and a known outcome metric at lag k; the metric is leading if peak correlation occurs at k > 0.
difficultyJustification: Requires historical time-series and a defined outcome variable.

name: Granularity / Aggregation Bias
shortDescription: Whether averaging hides important variance (e.g., a 60-min mean MTTR may include 4-hour outliers and 5-min recoveries).
measurement: Report distributions, not just means. Compare mean, median, p90, p99; disaggregate to check for Simpson's paradox.
difficultyJustification: Modern tooling makes this easy to compute; routinely ignored in executive dashboards.

name: Comparability / Cross-Team Validity
shortDescription: Whether the metric is meaningful when compared across teams, services, or organizations.
measurement: Define normalization (per-engineer? per-service? per-LOC?); evaluate context invariance via mixed-effects models.
difficultyJustification: Most engineering metrics fail this test (lines of code being the canonical example).

name: Cost of Measurement
shortDescription: Resources (compute, engineering time, intrusion) required to collect the metric.
measurement: Track instrumentation overhead (coverage instrumentation slows tests; APM sampling costs CPU); annotate metric inventory with a cost score.
difficultyJustification: Often forgotten until the data pipeline starts to break under its own weight.

name: Timeliness / Latency to Insight
shortDescription: How fresh the metric is when it reaches a decision-maker.
measurement: time(decision) - time(event), measured as the delay between the underlying event and when the metric becomes available to a decision-maker.
difficultyJustification: Often the difference between "actionable" and "post-mortem only."

name: Monotonicity
shortDescription: Whether the metric behaves consistently when its underlying construct changes (for example, adding a complex method should never decrease module complexity - Weyuker 1988).
measurement: Algebraic check against Weyuker's properties; empirical perturbation tests.
difficultyJustification: Well-defined for code metrics; less so for behavioral ones.
